coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of silane-assisted fluorescent carbon dots based on sanding dusts and its application in flame-retardant and anti-leaching wood materials
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Longfei
Peng, Limin
Liang, Shanqing
Chen, Zhilin
Lyu, Shaoyi
Wang, Siqun
coredata.dc:description: Novel silane-assisted flame-retardant carbon dots (FR-CDs) were synthesized via the hydrothermal carbonization strategy using recycled wood sanding dusts, tris(hydroxymethyl) phosphine oxide (THPO), phosphoric acid (PA), and amino-silane as precursors. The highly transparent FR-CDs suspension appears pale yellow color under daylight and exhibits a strong fluorescence response to light excitations, which helps to keep the original color of the wood and also has broad application prospects for monitoring the leaching behavior of FR-wood. The as-prepared FR-CDs can be applied in FR-wood materials using the facile dipping process owing to their tiny nanoparticles, uniform dispersion, and numerous nitrogen–phosphorus groups. In the flame test, the FR-CDs treated wood composites emitted little smoke initially and maintained primary carbon layer that did not catch fire for over 180 s. The flame-retardancy of the FR-wood materials was confirmed by the thermo-analysis, limiting oxygen index, exposure to a high heat flux (50 kW/cm2), and the propane blowtorch tests (∼600 °C). Moreover, the formation mechanism of the silane-assisted FR-CDs during hydrothermal process and the FR-wood materials with dipping method was proposed. This study provides a facile and efficient strategy for developing highly transparent and cheap flame retardants for the wood materials, which could expand the application of the wood sanding dusts and the silane-assisted carbon-based nanomaterials in the design of anti-leaching flame-retardant materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Wood
Recycling sanding dusts
Fluorescence
Flame retardants

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of HPL sanding dust extractions
Synthesis of silane-assisted FR-CDs with recycling pre-extractions
Preparation of flame-retardant wood materials via dipping process
Evaluation of wood surface color parameters
Flame retardant performance of wood functional materials
Structure and performance of fluorescent CDs
Combustion kinetics process of flame-retardant wood composites
Anti-leaching mechanism and surface chromaticity of flame-retardant wood composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Fundamental Research Funds of CAF (CAFYBB2022MB001) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32001261).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of fluorescent silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs and the green production of wood composites with flame-retardant and anti-leaching properties.

TEM and HRTEM images (inset) of (a) HPL-CDs, (b) PA@FR-CDs, and (c) THPO@FR-CDs; (d) 2D-AFM, (e) 3D-AFM image, and (f) AFM height profiles of PA@FR-CDs; (g) 2D-AFM, (h) 3D-AFM image, and (i) AFM height profiles of THPO@FR-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of different CDs. Fluorescence emission spectra of (b) HPL-CDs, (c) PA@FR-CDs, and (d) THPO@FR-CDs with different excitation wavelengths (Inset: photographs of CDs taken under visible light and a 365 nm UV-lamp).
(a) XPS survey spectra of CDs; (b) Enlarged Si2p of silane-assisted FR-CDs; High-resolution N1s spectra of (c) PA@FR-CDs and (d) THPO@FR-CDs; (e, f) P2p spectra of FR-CDs.

FTIR spectra and thermal analysis of raw stocks and FR-wood materials. (a) FTIR spectra of HPL, water pre-extracts (HPL-165L), and residual solid of the HPL particles (HPL-165S); (b) FTIR spectra of CDs; (c) TGA and (d) differential thermogravimetry (DTG) curves of different wood composites.

SEM images and EDS scans of wood materials. (a) Longitudinal section and (d, g) cross-section of control wood. Longitudinal sections of (b) PA@FR-CDs- and (c) THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood; Cross sections of (e, h) PA@FR-CDs- and (f, i) THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood materials; The element-mapping distributions of C, O, N, P, and Si for THPO@FR-CDs treated wood are shown in (j)–(n), respectively.

Snapshots and IR thermal screenshots of the burning experiments using a propane blowtorch. (a) Control wood, (b) PA@FR-CDs-treated wood, and (c) THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood in the time period of 0–180 s; IR of control wood before burning (d), and burning behavior of (e) control wood, (f) PA@FR-CDs- and (g) THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood at 180 s.
Heat release and smoke generation performances of control and FR-CDs-treated wood. (a) HRR, (b) THR, (c) SPR, and (d) TSP curves.

SEM images and EDS spectra of the char residue of THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood. SEM images of (a) longitudinal section and (b) cross section (inset: magnified images). (c) EDS curves and elemental contents; the distributions of C, P, N, and Si are shown in (d), (e), (f) and (g), respectively.

Anti-leaching behavior and surface chromaticity of wood materials. (a) LOI and (b) CA values (inset: the water droplets on the wood surface after a 5 s delay); (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the soaking solution (λex = 370 nm) and (d) surface color of the wood; (e)Anti-leaching property and (f) bending strength of FR-wood materials.
Possible mechanisms of silane-assisted N,P-doped fluorescent FR-CDs nanomaterials and their flame-retardant wood composites via the facile dipping process.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of silane-assisted fluorescent carbon dots based on sanding dusts and its application in flame-retardant and anti-leaching wood materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Wood Industry, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Beijing, 100091, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Wood Industry, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Beijing, 100091, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Renewable Carbon, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, 37996, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Renewable Carbon, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, 37996, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel silane-assisted flame-retardant carbon dots (FR-CDs) were synthesized via the hydrothermal carbonization strategy using recycled wood sanding dusts, tris(hydroxymethyl) phosphine oxide (THPO), phosphoric acid (PA), and amino-silane as precursors. The highly transparent FR-CDs suspension appears pale yellow color under daylight and exhibits a strong fluorescence response to light excitations, which helps to keep the original color of the wood and also has broad application prospects for monitoring the leaching behavior of FR-wood. The as-prepared FR-CDs can be applied in FR-wood materials using the facile dipping process owing to their tiny nanoparticles, uniform dispersion, and numerous nitrogen–phosphorus groups. In the flame test, the FR-CDs treated wood composites emitted little smoke initially and maintained primary carbon layer that did not catch fire for over 180 s. The flame-retardancy of the FR-wood materials was confirmed by the thermo-analysis, limiting oxygen index, exposure to a high heat flux (50 kW/cm2), and the propane blowtorch tests (∼600 °C). Moreover, the formation mechanism of the silane-assisted FR-CDs during hydrothermal process and the FR-wood materials with dipping method was proposed. This study provides a facile and efficient strategy for developing highly transparent and cheap flame retardants for the wood materials, which could expand the application of the wood sanding dusts and the silane-assisted carbon-based nanomaterials in the design of anti-leaching flame-retardant materials.

Fluorescent flame retardant carbon dots (FR-CDs) were synthesized with the sanding dust extraction and phosphorus sources as precursors. The dipping FR-wood with transparent FR-CDs can preserve the original color of wood and allow monitoring of the leaching behavior owing to the fluorescence activity.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainable development and low-carbon strategies have become vital models in the worldwide recently (Shadpour and Vajiheh, 2018; Jin and Chung, 2020). As an inevitable biomass resource in the mechanical processing of typical wooden furniture, flooring, doors and wood-based panels, the recycling utilization of wood sanding dust has attracted significant attention (Ockajova et al., 2010; Sisler et al., 2019). The annual output of wood sanding dust in China is more than 5–6 million tons. These wastes are generally recycled as materials for direct burning, manufacturing charcoal (Rabbat et al., 2023), and filler for the traditional wood-based panels (Huang et al., 2022), which is lack of the high-valued utilization strategy owing to the abundant lignocellulose materials and additives in the diverse wood sanding dust.

Hydrothermal carbonization process, as a common lignocellulose biomass refining method, has proven to be a high-valued, rich-carbon, and eco-friendly strategy (Gu et al., 2022; Gong et al., 2023). Compared with the regular one-step carbonization process at severe condition, our previous two-step method involving water pre-extraction at low temperature and carbonization at high temperature was confirmed to be a facile strategy to avoid the excessive carbonization of the whole lignocellulose biomass (Zhang et al., 2020). The biomass after pre-extraction at relatively low temperature can still be reused for compressing charcoal, heating generation, and fillers for wood-based panel industry. The hydrothermal pre-extraction liquid of the wood sanding dust include glycan, saccharide, and lignin condensation products (Zhang et al., 2020). These are essential precursors for the production of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), while do not need to carbonize the whole biomass that can be used for other purposes. Owing to their hyperfine particles with diameters smaller than 10 nm, stable fluorescence, and low toxicity, CDs have recently been developed to meet specific needs (Zhao et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2022), such as anti-bacterial additives (Travlou et al., 2018), ultraviolet (UV)-blocking particles (Hess et al., 2017; Gan et al., 2022), and photocatalytic materials (Liu et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2023). However, no one has considered the flame stability of the products in industrial applications, which inspires us to develop flame-retardant (FR) CDs in the wood industry.

Wood materials are naturally renewable, eco-friendly and most widely used in the furniture, floor, and ceiling areas (Mali et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). As typical flammable materials, wood materials possess a vast fire hazard owing to their flammability in residential houses and buildings (Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2022). Therefore, developing FR-wood materials that have few negative effects on human health and the indoor environment is the basic strategy to avoid such catastrophes owing to fire. Various flame retardants have been developed for construction materials (Shao et al., 2020; Mali et al., 2022), such as halogenated compounds (Zhang et al., 2017), ammonium polyphosphate (APP), carbon-based fiber (Kim et al., 2020), and other phosphates (Lu et al., 2020). However, such flame retardants in composites can easily leach into the environment and continually emit pollutants that can increase the surface chemical hazard of functional wood composites.

In recent years, materials containing nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) have been extensively applied and developed as efficient flame retardants (Xie et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2023). In particular, P-containing flame retardants are known to accelerate the yield of carbon residue, which acts as a barrier to extinguish trailing fires (Rahimi-Aghdam et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2021). In previous studies, a few studies have confirmed that P-doped graphene (Some et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2018) and carbon nanotubes (Shadpour and Vajiheh, 2018) can act as a good flame-retardant for cellulosic composites. Although these P-doped graphene units exhibit high flame retardancy, they are generally expensive, complicated to prepare, and difficult to strongly combine with the cellulose matrix. Furthermore, such black graphene units on wood coatings are normally restricted owing to the darkening of the original wood color. Thus, it is essential to develop transparent and applicable carbon-based flame retardants for wood materials. The recycled wood sanding dust of high-pressure laminates (HPL) materials has many fascinating advantages, including its abundance of melamine, cellulosic sugars, and aromatic lignin (Zhang et al., 2022). These carbon-based components with P and Si heteroatoms are expected to lead to good flame retardants while retaining the robust adhesion on wood fibers and additional characteristic fluorescence for anti-leaching evaluation.

Herein, we report a facile procedure to efficiently synthesize silane-assisted FR-CDs nanomaterials that can serve as highly effective N,P-doped flame retardants in FR-wood composites. The FR-CDs were prepared through hydrothermal treatment with wood sanding dust extracted from HPL as starting material and a phosphorous source as a carbonization accelerator. The N,P-doped FR-CDs solutions exhibited good fluorescence, excellent dispersion, and good biocompatibility. In addition, the pale-yellow color of the FR-CDs solution is amenable to retain the natural color of wood. To our knowledge, this is the first report of fluorescent N,P-doped CDs as transparent flame retardants that preserve the original color of the wood surface, while offering efficient protection without secondary fire generation. This study provides a facile and novel approach for silane-assisted N,P-doped flame retardants for wood materials using the dipping process, which could expand the application of recycled wood sanding dust and fluorescent silane-assisted N,P-doped nanomaterials in anti-leaching FR-wood composites.

Wood sanding dust from HPL was obtained from Zhejiang Ruixin Co., Ltd., China. Approximately 60 wt% of the sanding dust are comprising of particles with dimensions of 60–180 mesh (80–250 μm). Tris(hydroxymethyl) phosphine oxide (THPO; 85%) was purchased from Gaoming Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Water-soluble ammonium polyphosphate (APP; 78%) were purchased from Changfeng Chemical Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). Chinese fir wood (Cunninghamia lanceolata) was cut into pieces with a thickness of 6 mm and an air-dry density of 0.39 g/cm3. The moisture of wood was adjusted to 8.6%. [3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl] trimethoxysilane (AATMS) and phosphoric acid (PA; 85%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of HPL sanding dust extractions
ce:section.ce:para: Solid-liquid ratio of biomass and water has a significant effect on the yield of water extraction. Considering a relatively high extraction efficiency and good fluidity, sanding dust from the HPL panels and deionized water (150.0 g) were mixed in a solid-to-liquid weight ratio of 1/12.5. These mixtures were then transferred to sealed reactors (250 mL) with Teflon tubes, which were placed in an oven (165 °C) and heated for 1.0 h. This process was defined as pre-extraction. After pre-extraction, the suspension was filtered through a polyester film with a pore size of 0.45 μm. The extraction was stored in a sealed bottle for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silane-assisted FR-CDs with recycling pre-extractions
ce:section.ce:para: The pre-extraction solution (32.0 g) was mixed with a phosphorus source (THPO or PA, 1.6 g) and AATMS (0.46 g) and then sealed in a 50 mL autoclaves. The container was then filled with nitrogen gas to remove excess oxygen gas and transferred to an oven (220 °C for 6 h) for carbonization. The autoclave system was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the resulting aqueous solution was ultrasonically dispersed (200 W, 15 min) and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 8 min. Finally, the precipitate was dried to obtain N,P-doped FR-CDs, which were labeled as THPO@FR-CDs and PA@FR-CDs. The production yield of the FR-CDs was calculated according to the dry weight of extraction, phosphorus source, and AATMS. The yield for THPO@FR-CDs and PA@FR-CDs was 89.4% and 85.6%, respectively. The flowchart for the preparation of FR-CDs is shown in Fig. S1. The final FR-CDs products after vacuum drying is shown in Fig. S2. For comparison, the pre-extractions without additional P- and Si-containing chemicals were hydrothermal treated in the same process (220 °C for 6 h) and labeled as HPL-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of flame-retardant wood materials via dipping process
ce:section.ce:para: The N,P-doped FR-CDs and control HPL-CDs were mixed with deionized water to obtain a 10 wt% solution, respectively. The wood specimens were then immersed in this solution at room temperature for 6 h. The specimens were then removed and placed in a drying oven setting to 60 °C to remove the excess water. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the N,P-doped FR-CDs and FR-wood composites.

Morphology of the dispersed CDs was observed using a G2-F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI Tecnai, USA). The morphologies of CDs in three-dimensional (3D) and two-dimensional (2D) modes were obtained using an atomic force microscope (AFM; Bruker, Germany) in nano-probe modes. Topographies of the CDs coated on flat glass and wood chips were obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM; Hitachi 8020, Japan). Elemental mapping was performed using an energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) detector. Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a FLS100/FS5 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). The chemical compositions were tested using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo 250xi, USA). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired on a Nicolet IS10 spectrometer (USA). The water contact angle (CA) was measured using a contact angle tester (JC-200D, China) with a droplet of 5.0 μL. UV–vis spectra were processed using an E−60S spectrophotometer (Fisher Scientific, USA). Bending strength of the wood materials was carried out according to ISO 13061–3: 2014, the dimension of the specimens was 300 × 20 × 6 mm3 and the span of 240 mm was set for all the tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of wood surface color parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface colorimetric parameters of wood were measured using a surface colorimeter (CR-400, Minolta, Japan), including the lightness index (L*), red-green index (a*), and yellow-blue index (b*). The calculation for apparent color difference (ΔE) is shown in Equation (1).
(1)
△ E =
[
(
L *
– L
* 0
)
2
+
(
a *
– a
* 0
)
2
+
(
b *
– b
* 0
)
2
]
1 / 2

L*
0 , a*
0 , and b*
0 are the test values for control wood. L*, a*, and b* are the test values for the FR-wood.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant performance of wood functional materials
ce:section.ce:para: The flame retardancy was investigated by evaluating the limiting oxygen index (LOI), thermal analysis, exposure to high heat flux (50 kW/cm2), and a propane flame blown at high temperatures (∼600 °C). The LOI values were measured using an ignition analyzer (HC-2CZ, China). Thermal analysis was performed using an F5/F3 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA; STA449, Germany) in static air (20 °C/min). The combustion behaviors were evaluated using a cone calorimeter (CONE, UK) according to ISO 5660–2002. The sample thickness was 6 mm, and the heat flux was set to 50 kw/m2. The flame length in the propane blowtorch test was designated as ∼1.0 inch and located at the middle of the lower edge of the suspended sample. An IR thermal imaging camera (Fluke, USA) was used to capture the IR snapshots. The calorific values of oven-dry wood samples before and after FR-CDs treatment were tested by a flame calorimeter according to ISO 1716–2002. To intuitively evaluate the leaching resistance of the FR-wood, the samples were soaked in static water at 20 °C or at a high temperature of 65 °C for 24 h. The samples were fully dried for further tests to evaluate the leaching behavior of the FR-CDs on wood. For comparison, the anti-leaching behavior of FR-wood with traditional water-soluble APP was also examined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and performance of fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM morphologies were obtained to evaluate the sizes of the as-prepared HPL-CDs, PA@FR-CDs, and THPO@FR-CDs. The HPL-CDs (Fig. 2 a) were distributed in the 3–6 nm range, with an average size of ∼5 nm. In comparison with the TEM images of PA@FR-CDs (Fig. 2b) and THPO@FR-CDs (Fig. 2c), the silane-assisted N,P-doped CDs grew remarkably larger in the presence of an additional phosphorus source and AATMS modification. The sizes of the THPO@FR-CDs and PA@FR-CDs were distributed in the 8–15 nm range, with an average size of ∼10 nm. The lattice spacing in the HRTEM image of the three CDs is approximately 0.21 nm, indicating that the spacing array can be assigned to the crystalline phase of graphite (Fig. 2a–c inset), which is consistent with our previous report on grass-derived biomass CDs (Zhang et al., 2020). Moreover, the 2D-AFM maps (Fig. 2d and g) and 3D-AFM images (Fig. 2e and h) of FR-CDs also indicate the formation of well-dispersed dots and some large aggregations. Although the FR-CDs aggregate owing to strong silane-assisted effect to form big particles with size between 110 nm and 210 nm (Fig. 2f and i), a clear circular shape and the average height lower than 15 nm in the AFM height profiles of random dots were still identified.

UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3 a) of the control and FR-CDs were acquired to analyze their optical properties. The main UV–vis absorption peak of the HPL-CDs appeared at 280 nm, indicating a π–π* transition, which is similar to that of previous grass-derived CDs and may be related to the CC bond (Zhang et al., 2020). The FR-CDs gave rise to an absorption maximum at 225 nm, in addition to a peak at 285 nm. It is mainly attributed to the n–π* and π–π* transition of silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs. A red shift was observed for the enlarged absorption peak (285 nm) of the PA@FR-CDs and THPO@FR-CDs owing to the increase of CO structure after N,P-doped modification, which is in accordance with previous reports on P-doped carbon-based nanomaterials (Khose et al., 2018).

The fluorescence spectra of PA@FR-CDs and THPO@FR-CDs are displayed in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. The maximum emission wavelength of the PA@FR-CDs is centered at 475 nm and gives rise to a bright blue-green color, which can be clearly observed under an UV-lamp (λex = 365 nm, inset of Fig. 3c). THPO@FR-CDs produces a maximum emission wavelength centered at 545 nm (Fig. 3d) and appears bright green color under a 365 nm UV-light, which differs from the emission spectra of the HPL-CDs (Fig. 3b) without PA or THPO precursor. This is possibly due to the P-doping in the FR-CDs that increases the electron transition between the σ and π molecular orbital (Pan et al., 2022).

XPS was used to determine the chemical and elemental composition of the silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs. According to the XPS results shown in Fig. 4 a, the main peaks for the FR-CDs are centered at 102.35, 133.84, 153.27, and 189.23 eV, which are attributed to Si2p, P2p, Si2s, and P2s, respectively. However, the HPL-CDs only exhibited the typical C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks at 284.75, 401.32, and 531.76 eV, respectively. Moreover, the two kinds of FR-CDs were determined to be composed of C, N, O, P, and Si, which can be attributed to the incorporation of THPO/PA and AATMS. The enlarged Si 2p spectra of the silane-assisted FR-CDs reveal silicon at 102.8 eV originating from the Si–O–Si linkage (Fig. 4b). The counts of Si 2p for the THPO-modified FR-CDs (THPO@FR-CDs) were enhanced compared to the PA@FR-CDs. This confirms the successful grafting of AATMS onto the FR-CDs nanoparticles via the covalent bonding of siloxane with the hydroxyl groups on the FR-CDs.

The resolved peaks at about 399.25, 399.84, and 401.58 eV in the N1s spectra (Fig. 4c and d) correspond to the C–N–C, pyrrolic-N, and amino-N, respectively (Ayiania et al., 2020). The intensity of C–N–C in the PA@FR-CDs (Fig. 4c) was slightly higher than that in the THPO@FR-CDs (Fig. 4d), which was due to the strong catalysis and oxidization of PA during the pyrolysis reaction. Two highly resolved peaks in the P2p spectrum (Fig. 4e and f) occur at about 133.20, and 134.61 eV, which are assigned to the PO and P–C groups, respectively. The signal for the P–C groups in the spectrum of the THPO@FR-CDs (Fig. 4f) was more intense than that in the PA@FR-CDs (Fig. 4e), which is mainly due to the more abundant P–C bonds in the THPO. Therefore, the XPS confirmed the presence of N-, P-, and Si-assisted groups in the FR-CDs.

FTIR spectra of the HPL sanding dust, hot-water extracts (HPL-165L), and residual solid of the HPL particles (HPL-165S) are displayed in Fig. 5 a. The frequencies and assignments of the main signals for the biomass and different CDs are summarized in Table S1. In the case of the HPL-165L, the spectrum exhibited an obvious shoulder peak at about 2845 cm−1, which was mainly attributed to the enhanced –CH2 signal of holocellulose and lignin with the increased removal of easily extractable matters from the sanding dust (Rahimi-Aghdam et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the in-plane bending vibration of amine N–H at 1650 cm−1 and CN stretching vibration at 1550 cm−1 for the HPL-165L increased significantly (Li et al., 2016; Cheng et al., 2020). Furthermore, the typical signals for the HPL-165L at around 1150 cm−1 and 816 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of the triazine ring of C–N (Yuan et al., 2017; Zhan et al., 2020). This also provided obvious evidence that abundant N-containing matters were extracted into the pre-extraction precursors, which is essential for the production of intrinsic N-doped CDs (HPL-CDs) with further hydrothermal carbonization reaction.

In the case of different CDs (Fig. 5b), the broad peak around 3390∼3030 cm−1 is mainly attributed to the stretching vibration of–OH (Khose et al., 2018), indicating the existence of hydroxyl or carboxyl groups in the CDs. It was obvious that the hygroscopic behavior of THPO@FR-CDs distinctly decreased in comparison with that of the HPL-CDs and PA@FR-CDs. Meanwhile, the triazine-ring CN at 1469 cm−1 and C–N at 826 cm−1 for THPO@FR-CDs are more intense than those of the PA@FR-CDs, which is mainly attributed to the strong acidity of PA, while the CN and C–N structures were retained in the less acidic environment induced by the THPO. In addition, both N,P-doped CDs show a strong PO absorption at around 1142 cm−1. Furthermore, the P–C absorption peaks of PA@FR-CDs at 765 cm−1 was weak, which is consistent with previous studies on PA-modified CDs (Jiang et al., 2019). Moreover, combined with the XPS spectra, the broad signals at 1642 cm−1 are mainly attributed to the N–H in-plane vibration of the AATMS and extracts of the HPL sanding dust, which further increased the N-doped ratio in the FR-CDs.

The weight loss of the FR-wood materials was determined using TGA under air atmosphere. Fig. 5c displays the weight loss of four samples: control wood, HPL-CDs treated wood, PA@FR-CDs treated wood, and THPO@FR-CDs treated wood. Compared with the control wood, the wood treated with FR-CDs exhibited low thermal stability up to 280 °C. The rapid weight loss below 120 °C was mainly due to the adsorbed moisture in the wood. Compared with the DTG curve in Fig. 5d, the maximum weight loss peaks of FR-wood materials appeared earlier than those of control wood (about 345.2 °C), indicating that the addition of FR-CDs promotes the thermal decomposition at a lower temperature. The maximum weight-loss peaks of PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood underwent sharp reductions at 281.4 and 286.3 °C, respectively. Moreover, control wood also exhibited a sharp weight-loss peak at about 503 °C, while the FR-wood materials showed a broad weight-loss peak at 430–550 °C. This was mainly because the P-containing polymers in the FR-CDs promoted the dehydration of wood. As the temperature reached 700 °C, low residues was found for the control wood and HPL-CDs treated wood (∼0%), which is consistent with the thermal degradation behavior of wood in air atmosphere (Tomas et al., 2018). However, the PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood materials retained 14.3% and 15.3% of their mass, respectively. This further indicates that both the silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs with PA and THPO modification exhibited good char-forming behavior.

SEM was used to evaluate the surface morphologies of the FR-wood composites. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the surface of control wood contained a large number of pores, which is beneficial for the penetration of FR-CDs hyperfine nanoparticles. Compared with the relatively smooth surface of cell wall for the control wood (Fig. 6d–g) and HPL-CDs treated wood (Figs. S3 and S4), the microporous skeletons of the FR-wood in Fig. 6b and c were uniformly covered with FR-CDs particles. Moreover, the FR-CDs of PA@FR-CDs (Fig. 6e and h) and THPO@FR-CDs (Fig. 6f and i) occurred agglomeration on the wood skeleton, forming some bulges. This was mainly attributed to the active surface of N,P-dopants and silane-assisted groups on the wood skeleton.

Element-mapping of the FR-woods were investigated using EDS to identify the flame-retardant elements of the FR-CDs. For the THPO@FR-CDs treated wood (Fig. 6j-n and Fig. S5), the EDS diagrams and elemental analysis show that the FR-wood materials contain C, O, N, P, and Si in concentrations of 55.32, 38.89, 1.24, 3.68 and 0.87 wt%, respectively. The EDS diagrams and elemental contents of the PA@FR-CDs treated wood (Fig. S6) also indicate that N-, P-, and Si-dopants combined well with the wood. These results fully prove that the FR-CDs were successfully incorporated in the wood skeleton owing to the silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combustion kinetics process of flame-retardant wood composites
ce:section.ce:para: Both the control wood and FR-wood (dipping with FR-CDs) were simultaneously subjected to burning experiments in the air using high-temperature propane blowtorch at high temperature (∼600 °C) (Fig. 7 ). The PA@FR-CDs-treated wood (Fig. 7b) and THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood (Fig. 7m–r) emitted little smoke initially without catching fire, even after continuous heating up to 180 s (3 min). The wood well maintained its initial form, undergoing only a partial color change to black and slight swelling. Compared with the complete combustion and charring area of 100 cm2 in the control wood (Fig. S7), the FR-wood materials demonstrated a self-extinguishing ability, and the charring areas of the PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated woods were apparently reduced to 9.45 cm2 and 14.58 cm2, respectively (Fig. S7). It is mainly due to the formation of carbon resulting from heating the N,P-doped groups up to 300 °C, thus protecting the wood fibers beneath (Nazir et al., 2021). On the other hand, the control wood caught fire within 15 s and burned violently without an ignition source.

IR thermal screenshots of the woods were obtained during the propane-flame combustion tests. As shown in Fig. 7d, the temperature of the wood monitored in the air was about 18.6 °C. The control wood (Fig. 7e) burned violently after propane-flame ignition (15 s) and continued burning for about 180 s. The highest temperature (586.4 °C) occurred in the middle of the rough flame, and the smoke emitted from the upper end of the specimen maintained a relatively high temperature of 245.7 °C. For the FR-wood samples (Fig. 7f and g), the flame was significantly smaller than that of the control wood. It should be pointed out that unlike the control wood, which ignited for 15 s, the FR-woods were processed under a propane flame during the entire process (180 s). The maximum flame temperature occurred around the contact point between the propane flame and the wood edge, which was 605.8 °C and 593.4 °C for the PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated woods, respectively. The temperatures of the smoke emitted from the upper edge of the specimen were 116.2 °C and 108.4 °C, respectively. These values further indicates that the silane-assisted FR-CDs could impart an excellent flame-retardant effect.

Heat release and smoke generation performances of the control and FR-woods were evaluated using a cone calorimeter. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the heat release rate (HRR) of the FR-woods was significantly reduced by the FR-CDs dipping. The first peak of HRR curve decreased from 215.5 kw/m2 (control wood) to 176.9 and 167.4 kw/m2 for the PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood materials, respectively. The emergence time (about 15 s) of the first HRR peak remained unchanged for the three wood specimens. However, the second HRR peaks obviously decreased, with a clear time delay from 800 s to 1000–1140 s. This was mainly attributed to the thermal decomposition of the N,P-doped FR-CDs in the wood and could release volatile gases at high temperatures (Zhu et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2022). Furthermore, the PO group in the FR-CDs can further catalyze char formation on the wood surface, which is helpful for the delay and reduction of the HRR in the FR-wood composites.

As shown in the total heat release (THR) curve in Fig. 8b, the THR value of the control wood was higher than those of the FR-wood. The THR values at 600 s were 38.9, 20.2, and 16.0 MJ/m2 for the control wood, PA@FR-CDs-treated wood, and THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood, respectively. Furthermore, for the PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated woods, the THR at 1200 s was decreased by 37.1% and 45.2%, respectively, compared with the control wood, which is consistent with the downward trend of the HRR curves. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8c, the peak values of smoke production rate (SPR) of wood after the FR-CDs treatment were consistent with the changes in the HRR curves. However, the total smoke production (TSP) of the wood increased after the treatment with FR-CDs (Fig. 8d). This result is consistent with previous studies reporting increased smoke emission for carbon-based materials (Alongi et al., 2019). It should be pointed out that the smoke production of THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood was higher than that of control and PA@FR-CDs-treated wood, mainly owing to the breakage of C–P bond in FR-CDs, which promoted the dehydration of wood into char, thereby increasing the smoke production.

The char-residue morphology of the THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood composites was analyzed using SEM and EDS to evaluate the flame retardancy of the N,P-doped FR-CDs. The SEM images (Fig. 9 a and b) of the residual char after combustion reveal a dense structure and numerous carbon particles on the surface of the combustion interface, owing to the dehydration of the wood matrix and the aggregation of FR-CDs. Furthermore, the EDS diagrams and elemental contents (Fig. 9c–g) indicate that the char residue contains C, O, N, P, and Si at concentrations of 68.81, 29.08, 0.62, 2.65, and 1.04 wt%, respectively. The lower amount of nitrogen residue indicates that most of the nitrogen is released in the form of inert gases, and the higher amount of carbon residue indicates that the THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood composites form a thick, protective char layer after burning. For the PA@FR-CDs-treated wood composites (Fig. S8), the char residue were similar to those of the THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood. Their dense char residues and lower nitrogen contents confirm their ability to improve the flame retardancy of wood composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-leaching mechanism and surface chromaticity of flame-retardant wood composites
ce:section.ce:para: Under a high humidity or aqueous environment, the flame retardancy of the composites is generally weakened owing to leaching (Cheng et al., 2020). To verify the leaching resistance of the FR-wood with silane-assisted FR-CDs, the FR-CDs-treated wood and control wood samples were soaked in water at 20 °C or at a relatively higher temperature of 65 °C for 24 h. The specimens were then dried for the LOI test to determine the flame retardancy. As shown in Fig. 10 a, soaking in 20 °C water or warm water slightly reduced the LOI values of the FR-wood from 41.6% to 36.5% for PA@FR-CDs-treated wood and from 42.8% to 38.4% for the THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood. This indicates that most of the FR-CDs were retained in the wood skeleton. However, the calorific values of FR-wood were a little higher than that of control wood (Table S2), it was mainly due to the negative effect of increasing carbon-based compounds with the incorporation of FR-CDs (Walters, 2002). In addition, the higher content of P- and N-doping in FR-CDs was beneficial for the flame retardancy, which effectively inhibits the calorific values of FR-wood. Compared with the unsoaked FR-wood, the LOI values of PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood remained at 87.7% and 89.7%, respectively, after immersion in warm water (65 °C) for 24 h. It can also be seen from Fig. 10b that the CA values were 92.5o and 93.4o for the PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated woods, respectively. These contact angles were much higher than that of the control wood (62.4o) and can be attributed to the strong combination of Si–O bonds with the wood fibers. Moreover, the siliane-assisted FR-CDs hyperfine particles were distributed throughout the porous wood, which restrained the water penetration and further enhanced the anti-leaching behavior.

To confirm that the silane-assisted FR-wood had good leaching resistance in water, the fluorescence spectra of the FR-wood immersion liquids in 20 °C and warm water (65 °C) were acquired (Fig. 10c). For both FR-wood materials, the fluorescence emission peaks of the warm-water leaching solution were more intense than those of the normal-water (20 °C) leaching solution, indicating that warm water accelerated the leaching of small luminescent FR-CDs from the wood surface into water. Moreover, a slight redshift of the emission peak occurred, mainly owing to the agglomeration of the FR-CDs nanoparticles, which is consistent with a previous study that particle size affected the fluorescence spectra (Sun et al., 2021). To further evaluate the effect of FR-CDs on the color of the FR-treated wood, the surface chromaticity for both FR-woods is shown in Fig. 10d. Compared with the control wood, the surface brightness (L*) of the FR-wood slightly decreased by 2.57%–3.52%, and the red-green index (a*) and yellow-blue index (b*) increased by 16.43%–20.42% and 12.04%–20.66%, respectively. This was mainly attributed to the increase in roughness of the wood surface induced by the agglomerations between silane-assisted FR-CDs, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 6. In addition, the surface of the FR-wood was slightly darker than that of the control wood. However, compared with the control wood, the surface color difference (ΔE) of PA@FR-CDs- and THPO@FR-CDs-treated wood materials were only 3.89 and 4.93, respectively, which were similar to that of the control wood under observation by the naked eye. This also indicates that the wood surface color was unaffected by the silane-assisted FR-CDs treatment. Fig. 10e shows that both the FR-CDs had good anti-leaching ability compared to the traditional water-soluble APP, which is attributed to the coupling effect of silane-assisted FR-CDs. Moreover, the bending strength of FR-CDs-treated wood specimens was slightly higher than that of control wood (Fig. 10f). These results are very beneficial for the utilization of FR-CDs in FR-wood industry.

Furthermore, the cost comparison of different silane-assisted FR-CDs is shown in Table S3. The rough cost of FR-CDs was estimated for the ecofriendly chemicals without the consideration of the cost of wood sanding dust. The conversion cost for silane-assisted THPO@FR-CDs was about $4895.0 per ton, while the cost for silane-assisted PA@FR-CDs was $3272.5 per ton. Hence, it can be observed that the cost of FR-CDs is competitive compared with the traditional flame retardant in wood industry, while maintains good flame retardancy, water resistance, and anti-leaching behaviors.

Based on these results, a possible flame-retardant mechanism of the silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs for wood composites is presented in Fig. 11 . The mechanism proceeds as follows. The addition of silane-assisted FR-CDs increases the carbon residue rate and may release volatilized nitrogenous gases during combustion. Emitting inert ammonia gas can effectively lower the concentration of oxygen and remove central heat, which is confirmed in the IR thermal screenshots of burning experiments. In addition, a small amount of PA or THPO in the N,P-doped system can promote the dehydration, crosslinking, and carbonization of the wood matrix. In the combustion process, P-containing PA and THPO derivatives in the FR-CDs also promote wood fibril dehydration into charcoal, inhibiting the spread of burning and protecting the fibers underneath. Moreover, volatile PA and THPO derivatives can release P-containing free radicals to capture H· and OH· during the combustion (Salmeia et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2021). The flame retardant process is a combined effect of the condensed phase by P-doing and the gas phase by N-doping materials. Finally, the FR-wood composites form a continuous, tightly condensed structure with lower heat release, which is helpful for proving that the silane-assisted N,P-doped PA@FR-CDs and THPO@FR-CDs have a good flame-retardant effect on the wood composites.

In conclusion, a novel and green strategy was developed to synthesize fluorescent silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs as highly transparent flame retardants. These silane-assisted FR-CDs nanomaterials, which appear pale-yellow under daylight and exhibit a high fluorescence response upon light excitation, help preserve the original color of the wood and allow monitoring of the leaching behavior during the practical application. Several phosphorous sources were applied, and THPO was verified to be a more suitable additive than PA in the anti-leaching FR-wood materials owing to its lower fluorescence of the water-soaking solution. The FR-wood via a facile dipping process also showed high durability in the flame retardancy experiments. The treated FR-wood materials did not catch fire for more than 180 s (3 min) and maintained its initial form, whereas the control wood caught fire within 15 s and completely burned within 3 min into a thin black mass. These silane-assisted N,P-doped FR-CDs can be considered as highly transparent flame retardants for the wood composites owing to their incorporation of sustainable wood sanding dust extraction, highly reactive amino-silane, and eco-friendly phosphorus sources.
Longfei Zhang: Writing – original draft. Limin Peng: Validation. Shanqing Liang: Formal analysis. Zhilin Chen: Conceptualization. Shaoyi Lyu: Supervision. Siqun Wang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Fundamental Research Funds of CAF (CAFYBB2022MB001) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32001261).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2024.141417.
